literature.title: Literature & narrative theory
literature.subtitle: On why we read, and why the stories we tell each other matter.
literature.intro: I came to media studies through literature. My undergraduate years at the University of Dhaka were spent inside English departments and little magazines, reading postcolonial fiction and narrative theory. This folder holds the books, essays, and memories that still shape how I think about truth, narrative, and attention.

items.text: Rebecca Solnit — A Field Guide to Getting Lost
Italo Calvino — Six Memos for the Next Millennium

sections.title: A memory
sections.body: 2022. Presiding over the English Department Art and Literary Club at Dhaka University. We organised a weekly chai o kobita reading circle — Bangla poems in one hand, tea-stained photocopies of Seamus Heaney in the other. I learned more about cadence and silence in those evenings than in any seminar.

items.meta: On climate and the failure of literary imagination
Still the best 15 minutes on the politics of the image

spanish.title: Spanish & language learning
spanish.subtitle: Currently A2, aiming for B1 by end of 2026.
spanish.intro: I fell in love with Spanish late — through Bolaño, through Almodóvar, through a trip I haven't taken yet. Tracking my progress honestly, including the plateaus.

items.text: Language exchange on Tandem — Buenos Aires

sections.title: Why Spanish
sections.body: The short answer: I want to read 2666 in the original Spanish before I turn 30. The longer answer involves a Colombian journalist I interviewed in 2024 whose phrasing in English was exquisite but whose Spanish — I could tell from a single voice note — was a whole other octave. I wanted to hear her properly.

film.title: Film aesthetics
film.subtitle: On cinema as a way of looking, not just watching.
film.intro: Film has been my second education. I keep a running log of what I watch and what it taught me about framing, pacing, and truth.

sections.title: The frame that changed me
sections.body: Satyajit Ray's Pather Panchali — the scene where Apu and Durga see the train cut across the kaash flowers. I was fourteen, watching on a borrowed DVD with a scratched surface. The screen would stutter every few frames. Somehow that made it more real. It was the first time I understood that cinema could be a way of remembering things that had not yet happened.

items.text: TBS feature — On Ruben Östlund and the comedy of inequality
Notebook — staircase shots in Bangla cinema

photography.title: Photography & travel
photography.subtitle: Quiet frames from the places I get to stand still in.
photography.intro: I carry a Fujifilm X100V everywhere. Most of what I shoot I never publish. This folder is for the few I come back to — and for the trips behind them.

sections.title: A photograph I haven't shared
sections.body: A boy sleeping on a stack of newspapers outside the Paltan press market, 4:30 AM, November 2023. I was on an assignment about night-shift printers. I took one frame and put the camera down. It felt rude to take a second. Sometimes the ethics of the picture you don't take is more interesting than the one you do.

community.title: Community education
community.subtitle: Teaching, translating, and passing things along.
community.intro: Some of the most meaningful work I've done happens outside the newsroom — counselling students, editing SOPs for free, running OSINT workshops for fact-checkers. This folder gathers those threads.

items.text: Tools for Journalists — open OSINT resource hub

sections.title: Why I do this
sections.body: In 2019, a junior from my village in Kishoreganj showed me his IELTS speaking practice recording. He had memorised every answer word-for-word. His accent was perfect. His confidence was a performance. I realised most of our education system rewards that performance. So I started spending evenings helping students actually think in English rather than recite in it. That habit has never really stopped.

items.text: Verification basics — BBC Media Action alumni

philosophy.title: Philosophy & ethics
philosophy.subtitle: The slow questions behind the fast news cycle.
philosophy.intro: Journalism throws up ethical puzzles every week. This folder is where I try to sit with them properly — in conversation with epistemology, ethics of AI, and the Bengali humanist tradition.

sections.title: The question I keep failing to answer
sections.body: Is it ethical to publish a verified story if you know the algorithm will only amplify the first, most inflammatory paragraph? I've sat with this since 2023. It shapes how I now structure leads. It has not made me a faster writer. It has, I hope, made me a more honest one.

items.text: Algorithmic epistemic injustice in South Asian feeds
On the virtue of slowness in investigative work
